project.objective: When and where did Anders Monsen marry Unna Halsteinsdatter, and what does the marriage record show?

questions.question: When and where did Anders Monsen (FamilySearch ID LKFW-9XH) marry Unna Halsteinsdatter, and what does the marriage record show?
questions.rationale: This is the core objective of the project. Norwegian church marriage registers (kirkebøker) exist for most parishes from the 17th century onward and are widely indexed on FamilySearch and Digitalarkivet. A direct search for the marriage record is the logical first step and should yield the date, place, officiant, witnesses, and any parental information recorded.

exhaustive_declaration.justification: All 6 plan items completed. FamilySearch indexed Norway Marriages 1660-1926 (3 query variants), FTS (4 variants, coverage gap confirmed), volume search (negative), and FamilySearch 1801 census (positive — Manger household confirmed) were all searched via automated tools. Digitalarkivet.no Hamre parish, Manger parish church books, and Ancestry Norway Marriages were pursued but are inaccessible in an autonomous run (require interactive user browser access) and are logged as deferred leads. These are pursued-and-unavailable sources, not unsearched gaps. The accessible evidence — 1801 Manger census co-residence of Anders Monsen and Udna Halstensdatter (same_person score 0.923 for Udna) — supports a defensible if limited conclusion: the marriage occurred before ca. 1796 (Jørgen Andersen, b. ca. 1796, patronymic identifies Anders as father), in Hordaland, Norway. The exact marriage date, place, and record content cannot be established from currently accessible sources.

stop_criteria.goal_alignment: Partial — the marriage is confirmed by indirect evidence (co-residence in 1801 Manger census, child Jørgen Andersen b. ca.1796 whose patronymic identifies Anders as father). The marriage predates ca.1796. The exact date, place, and marriage record content remain unknown; the question 'what does the marriage record show?' cannot be answered from accessible sources.
stop_criteria.repository_breadth: Yes — FamilySearch indexed Norway Marriages 1660-1926 (3 variants); FamilySearch Norway Church Books volume browse; FamilySearch FTS corpus (4 variants — coverage gap confirmed for pre-1800 Hordaland); FamilySearch Norway Census 1801; Digitalarkivet.no Hamre parish church books (deferred, inaccessible in autonomous run); Digitalarkivet.no Manger parish church books (deferred, inaccessible); Ancestry Norway Marriages (deferred, inaccessible). All identified repositories searched or documented.
stop_criteria.original_substitution: Partial — FamilySearch indexed marriages (derivative) returned nothing; original church books (Digitalarkivet.no) are the primary substitution target but are inaccessible via automated tools and deferred. The 1801 census was accessed via FamilySearch indexed database (derivative). Original census images were not obtained but the indexed fact (co-residence with Udna, b.1745) is confirmed at same_person score 0.923.
stop_criteria.independent_verification: Not met — only one source (1801 Manger census) was found and extracted. All evidence for the marriage is indirect and from a single record. No independent direct or indirect corroboration of marriage date/place was accessible. This is the primary limitation of the conclusion.
stop_criteria.evidence_class: The 1801 census is a derivative source with indeterminate information quality. No original record with primary information about the marriage event was obtained. The marriage fact itself is inferred from co-residence. Unna's identity is confirmed by same_person score 0.923 (birth year exact match 1745=1745).
stop_criteria.conflict_resolution: No conflicts identified. Tree data (Couple relationship LKFW-9XH / KZHH-VTX) and census (co-residence 1801) are consistent. Birth year discrepancy for Anders (census 1756 vs. christening 1759) is a documented census age-rounding artifact, not a conflict.
stop_criteria.overturn_risk: Moderate for marriage date/place — the Digitalarkivet.no Hamre or Manger kirkebøk marriage register, if accessed in an interactive session, will likely supply the exact date and place (and possibly witnesses), overturning the 'unknown' finding on those specifics. The conclusion that the marriage occurred before 1796 in Hordaland is unlikely to be overturned. The deferred Digitalarkivet.no search is explicitly flagged as the highest-priority next step for an interactive session.

items.rationale: The FamilySearch 'Norway, Marriages, 1660-1926' collection (ID 1468080) is the largest indexed Norwegian marriage database, covering Hordaland parishes for this period. Anders (born 1759) and Unna (born 1745) likely married between 1770 and 1790. An indexed search on Anders Monsen is the fastest first step and should surface a record if it has been transcribed.

items.jurisdiction: Hamre parish (Den norske kirke), Hordaland, Norway
items.rationale: Digitalarkivet.no (Norwegian National Archives) hosts scanned and indexed kirkebøker for Hamre parish. Both Anders and Unna were christened at Hamre kirke, making Hamre the most probable parish for their marriage. Browsing the marriage (Viede) section of the Hamre ministerialbok for the 1770-1795 period is the highest-priority primary-source check. URL: https://media.digitalarkivet.no/en/kb/browse

items.jurisdiction: Manger parish (Den norske kirke), Hordaland, Norway
items.rationale: Anders died and was buried in Manger parish in 1821, indicating the family had settled there. It is possible the marriage also took place in Manger. Digitalarkivet.no should be searched for the Manger kirkebøk marriage (Viede) register for 1770-1795 as a parallel primary search alongside Hamre.

Ancestry hosts 'Norway Marriages, 1600s-1800s' (collection 1346) and 'Norway, Select Marriages, 1660-1927' (collection 60095), which overlap with but differ from the FamilySearch indexed set. If the FamilySearch indexed search (pli_001) returns nothing, Ancestry may have transcribed the same or additional records with better name matching.

FamilySearch 'Norway, Church Books, 1797-1958' (collection 4237104) contains 1.28 million images of Norwegian church books, some of which may include earlier volumes bound or catalogued into later books. Additionally, if the marriage was after 1797 it will be in this collection. A browse of available Hamre and Manger image groups is warranted if the indexed searches in pli_001 and pli_002 fail.

The 1801 Norwegian census (tilgjengelig på Digitalarkivet.no) lists all household members with ages and marital status. Finding Anders and Unna in the same household would confirm the marriage and could supply the approximate marriage year. Their children's ages in 1801 also help bound the marriage window. URL: https://www.digitalarkivet.no/en/census/search/1865 (comparable interface; 1801 census search at Digitalarkivet).

log.notes: 99 results returned but all with identical low scores (4.55). Anders Monsen appears as a secondary person (father/witness) in other couples' marriage records — not as the principal groom. Top match score from rank_search_matches was only 0.028, confirming no confident match. The groom's marriage entry is likely not indexed in this collection, or the record uses a farm name not captured in the index. Trying variant: search for Unna Halsteinsdatter as principal bride.

Single result returned: Anders Andersen + Halstensdr, married 19 Sep 1778 at Herøy, Møre og Romsdal — clearly a different couple. Groom's patronymic is Andersen (son of Anders) not Monsen (son of Mons), and the location is wrong county. False positive. Trying additional variants.

Single result: Anders Magnesen (not Monsen), spouse not named Unna. Different person. After 3 FamilySearch variant searches (log_001, log_002, log_003), no indexed entry for Anders Monsen + Unna Halsteinsdatter marriage found in collection 1468080. The record is likely not indexed in this FamilySearch collection — the original church book must be consulted via Digitalarkivet (pli_002/003).

query.target: Hamre parish (Den norske kirke) marriage register (Viede/Ekteviede), Hordaland, Norway
query.notes: Hamre parish (historically Hammer municipality, now Osterøy) held the relevant ministerialbok. Both Anders and Unna were christened at Hamre kirke. Browse the Viede (marriage) section of the Hamre ministerialbok for the 1770-1795 period.

log.notes: DEFERRED — autonomous run. Digitalarkivet.no requires a user to browse scanned church book images interactively; no automated capture is possible. URL for interactive session: https://media.digitalarkivet.no/en/kb/browse — navigate to Hordaland → Hamre (Hammer) parish → Ministerialbok → Viede section → 1770–1795 years. This is the highest-priority primary-source check since both individuals were christened at Hamre kirke.

query.target: Manger parish (Den norske kirke) marriage register (Viede), Hordaland, Norway

log.notes: DEFERRED — autonomous run. Anders died and was buried at Manger kirkegård in 1821, indicating the family resided in Manger parish. The marriage may have occurred there. URL for interactive session: https://media.digitalarkivet.no/en/kb/browse — navigate to Hordaland → Manger parish → Ministerialbok → Viede section → 1770–1795. Secondary to Hamre but important given Anders's later residence there.

query.scope: full FTS corpus (no collectionId, no place filter)

log.notes: 0 results. FamilySearch FTS corpus-wide co-occurrence search for both Norwegian patronymics together. Variant 1 of 4.

query.scope: full FTS corpus (no collectionId, no place filter)

log.notes: 2 results, both Belgian notarial records (Antwerp 1813, 1866) — false positives where 'monsen' is French abbreviation for Monseigneur and 'halstens' is a locality name. No Norwegian records. Variant 2 of 4.
0 results in Norway. Co-occurrence of Monsen + root stem Halstein scoped to Norway. Variant 3 of 4.

0 results. FamilySearch FTS coverage gap confirmed for pre-1800 Hordaland, Norway church books. 4 variants tried: +Monsen +Halsteinsdatter, +Monsen +Halstens, +Monsen +Halstein (Norway-scoped), +\"Anders Monsen\" +Unna (Norway-scoped). No AI-transcribed text of Hamre or Manger parish kirkebøker appears in the FTS corpus. The original record must be found via Digitalarkivet.no image browse (pli_002/003, deferred) or via FamilySearch volume image browse (pli_005). Variant 4 of 4 — coverage gap declared.

volume_search for Hordaland 1760-1800 returned 3 volumes, all covering Småland/Haus parish only (1771-1773) — wrong parish. No Hamre or Manger image groups found in FamilySearch for the pre-1800 period; 0 results at parish-level searches. FamilySearch does not have the Hamre/Manger kirkebøker digitized in a browse-accessible image group for this period. These records exist on Digitalarkivet.no (pli_002/003, deferred).

DEFERRED — autonomous run. Ancestry.com requires authenticated user browser session; no automated capture possible. URL: https://www.ancestry.com/search/collections/1346/?name=Anders_Monsen&spouse=Unna_Halsteinsdatter&event=_Norway_&event_year=1775-1790. Fallback for pli_001 (FamilySearch indexed search, exhausted).

DEFERRED — autonomous run. Digitalarkivet 1801 census requires user browser session. URL for interactive session: https://www.digitalarkivet.no/en/census/search/1801 — search for Anders Monsen in Hordaland. Would confirm household composition and children's ages, helping bound the marriage date.

query.note: Ad-hoc search to find household evidence of Anders Monsen and Unna Halsteinsdatter

log.notes: Top match (score 0.66) at Hamre, Hordaland — Anders Monsen in Nils Andersen household, no Unna. Second Hordaland record (rank 3, score 0.22) at Manger, Hordaland (ark:/61903/1:1:8YMK-6G3Z): household contains Anders Monsen (b.1756) and Udna Halstensdatter (b.1745) — clear match to Unna Halsteinsdatter. Also present: Ingebor Nielsdatter (b.1765) and Jørgen Andersen (b.1796). Confirms couple together in Manger 1801; does not provide marriage date or place. Passing Manger record to record-extraction.

sources.citation: "Norges folketelling, 1801," database, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:8YMK-6G3Z : accessed 9 July 2026), Anders Monsen household, Manger, Hordaland, Norway.

citation_detail.who: Anders Monsen, Udna Halstensdatter, Ingebor Nielsdatter, and Jørgen Andersen
citation_detail.what: Norway 1801 census household record (Norges Første Folketelling)
citation_detail.where_within: Collection 3733603; record ARK: https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:8YMK-6G3Z; household titled 'Household of Ingebor Nielsdatter', Manger, Hordaland, Norway

sources.notes: Indexed database compiled from the original 1801 Norwegian census (Norges Første Folketelling). FamilySearch collection 3733603 derives from Digitalarkivet.no's transcription. Birth years are inferred from ages recorded. The FamilySearch record title 'Household of Ingebor Nielsdatter' reflects the search principal; the actual household head role in the original requires image examination. Four persons in household: Anders Monsen (b. ca. 1756), Udna Halstensdatter (b. ca. 1745), Ingebor Nielsdatter (b. ca. 1765), Jørgen Andersen (b. ca. 1796).

assertions.informant: unknown household member (likely self or spouse)
assertions.informant_bias_notes: Pre-1940 census: respondent identity not recorded by the enumerator. Any household member (self, spouse, or other) could have provided the name. Because the specific respondent is unknown, information quality is indeterminate rather than primary, even though the name is a fact the subject and any household member would know correctly.

Pre-1940 census: respondent unknown. Per GPS classification standards, a subject's own age/birth year in a pre-1940 census is indeterminate (not secondary) because we cannot identify who answered — we cannot say the informant lacked first-hand knowledge. Note 3-year discrepancy with christening record (1759 vs. ca. 1756): age-rounding error is common. Christening record (primary) is more reliable.

The census enumerator visited the dwelling and recorded the residence; this is a witnessed fact. Residence in Manger in 1801 is indirect evidence for q_001 (marriage date/place) — it places both parties in Manger but requires inference to connect to a marriage location (which may have been Hamre or Manger, predating this census by years).

assertions.value: Co-resides with Udna Halstensdatter in Manger, Hordaland household in 1801 — circumstantial evidence of spousal relationship
assertions.informant_bias_notes: Co-residence does not prove marriage. However: ages (Anders b.ca.1756, Udna b.ca.1745) are consistent with a spousal couple; Jørgen Andersen (b.ca.1796) in the household has patronymic 'Andersen' = son of Anders, implying paternity; no alternative relationship is plausible given the ages. Marriage not stated in the census — this is indirect evidence only.

assertions.informant: unknown household member (likely self or spouse)
assertions.informant_bias_notes: Pre-1940 census: respondent identity not recorded. Any household member could have provided Udna's name. Information quality is indeterminate. Name variant 'Udna Halstensdatter' vs. 'Unna Halsteinsdatter' (tree) is consistent with Norwegian handwriting and indexing variation — the birth year match (1745) strongly corroborates identity.

Pre-1940 census: respondent unknown. A household member (possibly Udna herself) provided the age, but since the specific respondent is unknown, information quality is indeterminate. The ca. 1745 census birth year exactly corroborates the christening record (27 May 1745), strengthening identification.

Enumerator visited and recorded residence — primary for the residence fact. Evidence type is indirect for q_001: Udna's presence in Manger in 1801 does not directly state when or where she married; inference is required (she and Anders were in Manger together, implying settlement there, but the marriage predates this record by years).

Pre-1940 census: respondent unknown. A household member (likely a parent) provided Jørgen's age, but since the respondent is unidentified, information quality is indeterminate. The patronymic 'Andersen' (son of Anders) connects Jørgen to Anders Monsen and provides a terminus ante quem for the marriage of approximately 1795–1796.

person_evidence.rationale: Census record lists 'Anders Monsen' (Male, b. ca.1756) at Manger, Hordaland, 1801. Tree person LKFW-9XH is Anders Monsen (Male, christened 7 Apr 1759, buried 8 Jan 1821 at Manger kirkegård). Name is an exact match. Burial parish (Manger) exactly matches census residence parish. Birth-year discrepancy is 3 years (1756 census vs. 1759 christening) — well within normal census age-reporting error for 18th-century Norwegian records. Household includes 'Udna Halstensdatter' (b.1745), who matches tree spouse KZHH-VTX at same_person score 0.923, independently confirming household identity. same_person score 0.219 is depressed by the birth-year discrepancy but the qualitative correlation is strong on all non-age identifiers.

Census birth year ca.1756 for the same 'Anders Monsen' at Manger who is identified as LKFW-9XH. The 3-year discrepancy with the christening record (1759) is a documented census inaccuracy, not a contradiction. Links to LKFW-9XH on the same grounds as a_001.

Residence at Manger, Hordaland in 1801 for Anders Monsen (LKFW-9XH). Consistent with Anders's death and burial at Manger in 1821, showing sustained residence in this parish. The enumerator witnessed this residence directly.

Relationship assertion bears on Anders Monsen (LKFW-9XH) as the husband co-residing with Udna Halstensdatter. The co-residence in Manger 1801 is indirect evidence of the couple's marriage predating the census. Anders is identified with LKFW-9XH by name, location, and spouse match. Also linked to KZHH-VTX (Udna's side) in a separate pe_ entry.

Relationship assertion also bears on Udna Halstensdatter (KZHH-VTX) as the wife co-residing with Anders Monsen. 'Udna Halstensdatter' (b.1745) in the census matches KZHH-VTX 'Unna Halsteinsdatter' (christened 27 May 1745) at same_person score 0.923 — exact birth-year match and same-period Norwegian name variant. Co-residence with Anders implies the spousal relationship.

Census lists 'Udna Halstensdatter' (Female, b.1745) at Manger, Hordaland. Tree person KZHH-VTX is 'Unna Halsteinsdatter' (Female, christened 27 May 1745, Hamre, Hordaland). same_person score 0.923 (strong). Birth year is an exact match (1745 = 1745). Name variant 'Udna/Udna' vs 'Unna' and 'Halstensdatter' vs 'Halsteinsdatter' are standard Norwegian handwriting and indexing variants. Co-resident with Anders Monsen (LKFW-9XH), who is KZHH-VTX's tree spouse.
Census birth year 1745 for Udna Halstensdatter exactly corroborates Unna Halsteinsdatter's christening in May 1745. Links to KZHH-VTX on the same grounds as a_005.
Residence at Manger, Hordaland in 1801 for Udna Halstensdatter (KZHH-VTX). Consistent with her co-residence with Anders Monsen (LKFW-9XH) who was buried in Manger in 1821.

Census lists 'Jørgen Andersen' (Male, b. ca.1796) in the Manger household with Anders Monsen and Udna Halstensdatter. Patronymic 'Andersen' (= son of Anders) strongly suggests he is a biological son of Anders Monsen. No existing tree person matches; stub I1 created. Confidence is probable (single record, no independent corroboration yet) rather than confident. The parentage relationship is not asserted in the census itself — it is inferred from the patronymic and household co-residence.

proof_summaries.exhaustive_search_summary: FamilySearch indexed Norway Marriages 1660-1926 (collection 1468080) was searched in 3 query variants — all returned false positives where Anders Monsen appeared as a secondary party, not as the groom. FamilySearch full-text search was attempted in 4 co-occurrence variants across the full FTS corpus; zero relevant results confirmed a coverage gap for pre-1800 Hordaland church books in the FTS corpus. Volume search for Hamre and Manger parish image groups returned only Haus/Småland parish volumes. An ad-hoc FamilySearch census search (Norway Census 1801, collection 3733603) found the Manger household record confirming co-residence of Anders Monsen and Udna Halstensdatter. Digitalarkivet.no church books for Hamre parish (Viede section, 1770-1795) and Manger parish (Viede section, 1770-1795) — the highest-priority primary sources — were documented as deferred leads requiring interactive browser access unavailable in this autonomous run. Ancestry Norway Marriages collections were also deferred. No direct marriage record for this couple was found in any accessible source.
proof_summaries.narrative_markdown:
# Marriage of Anders Monsen and Unna Halsteinsdatter

**Conclusion tier: Probable**

## Research Question

When and where did Anders Monsen (FamilySearch ID LKFW-9XH, born 7 April 1759 at Hamre kirke, Hordaland, Norway; died 1821, buried 8 January 1821 at Manger kirkegård, Hordaland, Norway) marry Unna Halsteinsdatter (FamilySearch ID KZHH-VTX, christened 27 May 1745 at Hamre, Hordaland, Norway), and what does the marriage record show?

## Conclusion

The preponderance of available evidence strongly suggests that Anders Monsen and Unna Halsteinsdatter were married before approximately 1796, almost certainly in Hordaland, Norway — most likely in Hamre or Manger parish, where both were christened and where Anders later died. The exact marriage date, officiant, and witnesses remain undetermined: the original marriage register in the Hamre or Manger kirkebøk at Digitalarkivet.no has not been searched and is the primary outstanding lead. This conclusion is at the **probable** tier and cannot reach “proved” without access to the direct marriage record.

## Identity Confirmation: Udna Halstensdatter = Unna Halsteinsdatter

The 1801 Norwegian census (Norges Første Folketelling, derivative source, FamilySearch collection 3733603; record ARK: ark:/61903/1:1:8YMK-6G3Z; accessed 9 July 2026) records “Udna Halstensdatter” (Female, born ca. 1745) in a household at Manger, Hordaland, Norge. FamilySearch’s same_person algorithm returned a match score of 0.923 (confidence 5/5) between this census persona and tree person KZHH-VTX (“Unna Halsteinsdatter”, christened 27 May 1745, Hamre, Hordaland). The match is grounded in an exact birth-year correspondence — 1745 in the census and 1745 from the christening record. The name variant (“Udna Halstensdatter” vs. “Unna Halsteinsdatter”) is a standard Norwegian handwriting and indexing variation requiring no adjustment to the identification. The census birth year for this person is classified as indeterminate information quality (pre-1940 census, respondent unknown per GPS standards).

## Co-Residence as Indirect Evidence of Marriage

The same 1801 census record places “Anders Monsen” (Male, born ca. 1756) in the same Manger, Hordaland household as Udna Halstensdatter. The enumerator visited the dwelling and recorded the household members (primary information for the residence fact itself). Co-residence of a man and woman in an 1801 Norwegian household is not a direct statement of marriage — the marital status column in this indexed record does not carry a value for these persons — but it constitutes strong indirect evidence of a spousal relationship. No alternative relationship (sibling, parent-child, or unrelated lodger) is plausible given the ages of the two parties and the presence of an apparent son (see below). The same_person score for Anders Monsen (census) versus LKFW-9XH (tree) was 0.219 — below the moderate threshold of 0.4 — but the tool returned matched: true. The depressed score reflects the 3-year birth-year discrepancy (census: ca. 1756; christening record: 7 April 1759), a discrepancy typical of age-rounding in 18th-century Norwegian census records and well within the documented inaccuracy range for this source class. All non-age identifiers independently support the identification: name (“Anders Monsen”, exact); burial parish (tree: Manger kirkegård, 8 January 1821; census: Manger, Hordaland); and co-resident spouse confirmed at 0.923. The low score is treated as a census artifact rather than a genuine mismatch.

## Terminus Ante Quem: Jørgen Andersen (b. ca. 1796)

A third household member, Jørgen Andersen (Male, born ca. 1796), is present in the 1801 Manger census record. The Norwegian patronymic naming convention — “Andersen” meaning “son of Anders” — identifies Anders Monsen as Jørgen’s probable father. If this patronymic identification is correct, Anders and Unna’s marriage must predate Jørgen’s birth by at least nine months, placing the marriage no later than approximately 1795–1796. Jørgen’s birth year is derived from his age in the 1801 census (derivative, informant unidentified, classified indeterminate); his parentage is inferred from the patronymic and household co-residence, not stated explicitly in the census record.

## What Was Searched

**FamilySearch indexed Norway Marriages, 1660–1926 (collection 1468080):** Three query variants searched. 99 results on the first query were examined but all were false positives where “Anders Monsen” appeared as a secondary party (witness or father of bride/groom) in other couples’ marriage records. No indexed entry for this couple as principals was found. The highest ranked match score returned by rank_search_matches was 0.028, confirming no genuine candidate.

**FamilySearch full-text search (FTS corpus, ∼1.95 billion document images):** Four co-occurrence queries were executed across the full FTS corpus without collection scoping: +Monsen +Halsteinsdatter; +Monsen +Halstens; +Monsen +Halstein (Norway-scoped); +“Anders Monsen” +Unna (Norway-scoped). All returned zero relevant results. This confirms that FamilySearch’s AI-transcribed text corpus does not include the Hamre or Manger parish kirkebøker for the pre-1800 period — a coverage gap, not a negative finding.

**FamilySearch Norway Church Books, 1797–1958, volume search:** Three volumes were returned for Hordaland 1760–1800, all covering Haus/Småland parish. No browse-accessible image groups for Hamre or Manger exist in FamilySearch for the target period.

**FamilySearch Norway Census, 1801 (collection 3733603):** Ad-hoc search found the Manger household record (ark:/61903/1:1:8YMK-6G3Z) confirming co-residence.

## What Was Not Searched (Deferred Leads)

**Digitalarkivet.no — Hamre parish church books (Viede/marriage register, 1770–1795):** Both Anders and Unna were christened at Hamre kirke; this is the highest-priority source for the marriage record. Search was deferred in this autonomous research run because Digitalarkivet.no requires interactive browser access. This is the recommended first action in an interactive session: navigate to https://media.digitalarkivet.no/en/kb/browse → Hordaland → Hamre (Hammer) parish → Ministerialbok → Viede section → 1770–1795.

**Digitalarkivet.no — Manger parish church books (Viede register, 1770–1795):** Anders died in Manger in 1821; the marriage may have taken place in Manger if the couple had settled there before the target period. Also deferred.

**Ancestry Norway Marriages (collections 1346 and 60095):** Deferred (authenticated browser access required).

## Limitations

This conclusion rests on a single derivative source (the 1801 census, an indexed database) that provides only indirect evidence of the marriage. No independent corroboration of the marriage date or place was found in any accessible source. The marriage date and place are entirely unknown. Overturn risk for the specific date and place is high: the Digitalarkivet.no Hamre or Manger kirkebøk marriage register, once searched in an interactive session, is expected to supply a direct marriage record that will specify the date, officiant, and possibly witnesses, and will upgrade this conclusion from probable to proved.

## GPS Component Assessment

- **Component 1 (Exhaustive research):** Declared with limitations. All automated-tool sources exhausted; two primary sources deferred as inaccessible in autonomous context.
- **Component 2 (Complete citations):** The 1801 census citation is complete. Deferred sources are documented with URLs.
- **Component 3 (Analysis and correlation):** Assertions classified (a_001–a_008); person-evidence links created (pe_001–pe_009); same_person scores obtained for both principals.
- **Component 4 (Conflict resolution):** No conflicts identified. Birth-year discrepancy for Anders (3 years) is a census artifact, not a conflict between sources.
- **Component 5 (Written conclusion):** This summary.

**Tier: Probable.** All five GPS components are incomplete in at least one respect (independent verification absent; direct marriage record not obtained; derivative source only). The conclusion may be upgraded to “proved” when the Digitalarkivet.no church books for Hamre and Manger parishes are searched interactively and a direct marriage record is found and extracted.
